does: This paper identifies fundamental definitional problems in machine unlearning approaches. It demonstrates that the standard definition for approximate unlearning is flawed because models trained on different datasets can be identical, meaning one could claim unlearning without actually modifying model parameters. The authors then examine exact unlearning methods and show that even with a fixed training trajectory, it is impossible to formally prove the absence of data influence on a model. These theoretical results reveal critical gaps between how unlearning is currently defined and verified versus what would constitute genuine data forgetting in deep learning systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on machine unlearning that emerged from privacy regulations like GDPR's right-to-be-forgotten. It engages with two established research tracks: exact unlearning through retraining from scratch and approximate unlearning methods designed to save computation. The paper extends prior work on formalizing unlearning guarantees by critically examining the mathematical foundations underlying both approaches. It connects to broader research on model interpretability, data influence, and privacy verification in machine learning, while responding to the gap between intuitive notions of forgetting and rigorous formal definitions.
enables: This work motivates the development of new auditable and formally verifiable definitions of machine unlearning that go beyond simple model-distance metrics. It suggests future research must either establish alternative verification procedures that account for the non-uniqueness of model parameters or develop stronger mathematical frameworks for proving data influence elimination. The paper opens directions for designing unlearning mechanisms with inherent auditability properties and for establishing certification procedures that regulators and auditors could use to verify privacy compliance. It also points toward investigating whether exact unlearning is theoretically achievable under more refined definitions, potentially reshaping how privacy-preserving machine learning is both implemented and validated.
